html:
Johan II is the name of several rulers:

  • Ivan Asen II, Tsar of Bulgaria, 1218–1241
  • John II of Brabant, Duke of Brabant, 1294–1312
  • John II of France, King of France, 1350–1364
  • John II of Castile, Regent of Castile, 1406–1454
  • Ivan II of Moscow, Grand Prince of Moscow, 1353–1359
  • John II of Portugal, King of Portugal, 1481–1495
  • Hans (union king), King of Denmark, 1481–1513, of Norway, 1483–1513, and of Sweden, 1497–1501; in Sweden he is also known as John II, because his name in Latin is Johannes.
  • John II Casimir, King of Poland, 1648–1668

How certain are we about this information?

For people from the Middle Ages, there are often contemporary written sources (letters, chronicles, church documents), so the main facts are usually well-documented. However, these sources were mostly written by the Church and those in power, and their preservation is uneven, which can distort and limit our understanding of the past.
tldr: The name "Johan II" has been used by many different kings and leaders in various countries throughout history. While it's often easy to find basic information about them, certain details can be difficult to ascertain precisely because they lived so long ago.
lattlast:
Throughout history, several individuals have been named Johan II. These were leaders in various countries and eras, including Bulgaria, France, Portugal, and Sweden. In Sweden, he is also known as Hans, as his name "Johannes" translates to John in Latin.

We know a great deal about these individuals because old letters and texts from the church and those in power have been preserved over the centuries. These documents help us understand the significant events in their lives.

However, because these sources are so old, they can sometimes be difficult to interpret accurately. Since the historical accounts were primarily written by the church and those in positions of authority, certain details may be missing or described in a way that provides an incomplete picture of these individuals.
